macro_segments.title: The Debate on Whether One Condemned to Execution Incurs Obligations: Vows, Erech, and Sanctification versus Damages

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishnah: Rabbi Yosei's Position on Vows, Erech, and Sanctification by the Condemned
The Tanna Kamma's Clarification: The Debate Concerns Damages, Not Vows or Sanctification
The Core Issue: Does One Condemned to Execution Have to Pay Compensation for Property Damage?

macro_segments.title: The Reframing of the Debate: From Personal Legal Status to the Question of Debt Collection from Heirs

micro_segments.title: Rav Yosef's Explanation: The Debate Centers on Whether an Oral Loan (or Debt without a Writ) Can Be Collected from Heirs
The Tanna Kamma versus Rabbi Yosei: Whether an Oral Loan Can Be Collected from Heirs
Rava's Alternative: A Debt Written in Torah (Obligation to Pay for Damage) May Have Greater Weight Than a Regular Oral Debt

macro_segments.title: The Tosefta Parallel: Execution and Personal Injury—Can One Condemned to Execution Cause Injury to Others?

micro_segments.title: The Tosefta: One Going Out to Be Executed Who Causes Bodily Harm to Others
Rabbi Shimon ben Elazar's Position: One Condemned Is Exempt Because He Cannot Stand Trial
Rav Yosef's Reframing of This Debate Too: Same Issue of Oral Debt and Collection from Heirs

macro_segments.title: Bringing a Sacrifice for One Condemned to Execution: Is Postponement of Execution Forbidden?

micro_segments.title: The Principle of Not Postponing Execution (Einui HaDin): Bringing Sacrifices Before Execution
When a Condemned Person Sins During Execution: Whether to Bring an Offering
Abaye's Challenge: If We Don't Postpone Even for Atonement, Why Postpone for Past Sins?
Resolution: Postponement Only Allowed if the Sacrifice Is Already Slaughtered

macro_segments.title: Execution of a Pregnant Woman: The Status of the Fetus and the Principle Against Postponement

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah: A Pregnant Woman Condemned to Execution Is Not Delayed Until Birth
The Exception: If She Is Already in Labor, Execution Is Postponed Until After Delivery
Rashi's Explanation: The Fetus Gains Independent Identity During Childbirth

macro_segments.title: The Gemara's Three-Word Difficulty on Abortion: Pshita Gufa Hi (Of Course It Is Her Body)

micro_segments.title: The Opening of the Gemara: 'Pshita Gufa Hi'—The Fetus Is Simply Part of Her Body
The Potential Objection: The Father's Property Interest in the Fetus
The Scriptural Response: 'Both Shall Die'—Including the Child—Rejecting the Father's Claim

macro_segments.title: Woman in Labor Condemned to Execution: The Halachic Obligation to Abort the Fetus Before Execution

micro_segments.title: The Practice: Aborting the Fetus Before Executing the Mother to Prevent Degradation of the Corpse
The Reason: Preventing Nivul Ha-Meis (Degradation of the Corpse) If the Fetus Thrashes After Mother's Death
The Gemara's Discussion: Does the Fetus or Mother Die First in Natural versus Violent Death?

macro_segments.title: Performing a Cesarean Section on Shabbat to Save a Fetus When the Mother Dies in Labor

micro_segments.title: The Halacha: Permissible to Cut Open the Mother's Womb on Shabbat to Save the Fetus
The Reasoning: Cutting into a Dead Body Is Not a Melachah on Shabbat
The Chiddush: You Can Violate Shabbat Even for a Fetus That Has Never Established a Status of Life
The Parallel: Even Building Collapse Cases Allow Shabbat Violation for Uncertainty of Life

macro_segments.title: Benefit from the Executed Woman's Hair: Is Hair Part of the Body or Separate Property?

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah: Hair of an Executed Woman Is Not Forbidden for Benefit
Rav's Distinction: The Case Concerns Only Woven-In Hair, Not Natural Hair
The Question of Rabbi Yosei B'Rabbi Chanina: The Hair of Righteous Women in an Apostate City
Rava's Clarification: We Are Discussing Woven-In Hair That Hangs by a Peg
Rav Nachman Bar Yitzchak's Resolution: Hair Is Permitted Because Hair Does Not Die
The Distinction with Animals: An Animal's Entire Body Becomes Forbidden by the Court's Ruling, But a Woman Is Not Forbidden In Her Lifetime

Melachah on Shabbat (Violating Shabbat for Life)
